attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the general-practice clinic with concerns about mood changes. She reports irritability, anxiety and low mood that begin roughly one week before her period and resolve within a few days of menstruation starting. During this pre-menstrual week she struggles to concentrate at work, loses interest in usual activities and feels overwhelmed; on two occasions she has taken a few days off work, but she has never needed time off at any other point in the cycle. For the rest of each month she describes her mood and energy as normal. These symptoms have recurred for the past eight months and are placing significant strain on her relationships. She also recalls brief spells of increased energy and productivity lasting two to three days, yet denies late-night activity, impulsive spending or an unusually elevated mood during those episodes.

She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She reports a family history of depression and anxiety disorders.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Mental state examination reveals no current mood symptoms or thought disturbances, but she expresses concern about her emotional instability and its impact on her life.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis for this patient is premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is characterised by severe mood and physical symptoms that occur during the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle (typically one to two weeks before menses) and resolve shortly after the onset of menstruation. The patient's symptoms, including irritability, anxiety, depressed mood, difficulty concentrating, and decreased interest in activities, are consistent with premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Additionally, these symptoms have been present for at least two menstrual cycles (in this case, eight months) and are causing significant interference with her work and relationships, which are key diagnostic criteria for premenstrual dysphoric disorder.

Major depressive disorder typically involves persistent depressive symptoms lasting at least two weeks, without the cyclical pattern associated with the menstrual cycle seen in this patient. While some symptoms overlap with premenstrual dysphoric disorder, the clear relationship to the menstrual cycle and resolution of symptoms after menstruation make major depressive disorder less likely. However, the family history of depression might make this diagnosis more plausible.

Generalised anxiety disorder is characterised by excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least six months. While anxiety is a symptom of premenstrual dysphoric disorder, the cyclical nature of the symptoms and the presence of other mood symptoms make generalised anxiety disorder less likely in this case. The family history of anxiety disorders could make this diagnosis more considerable.

Bipolar II disorder requires at least one hypomanic episode lasting ≥ 4 consecutive days, featuring elevated or irritable mood plus symptoms such as decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, racing thoughts or impulsive risk-taking, and at least one major depressive episode—both of which must occur independently of the menstrual cycle as per the DSM-5 criteria. In this vignette every disabling mood symptom is confined to the late luteal phase and resolves within days of menstruation; she remains euthymic for the rest of the month and has never taken time off work outside that window, indicating no cycle-independent depression. Her brief “energy bursts” last only 2–3 days, fall short of the DSM duration threshold, and lack hypomanic hallmarks like reduced sleep or reckless behaviour. This predictable, hormone-linked pattern matches premenstrual dysphoric disorder, making bipolar II highly improbable.

Borderline personality disorder is characterised by a pervasive pattern of instability in interpersonal relationships, self-image, and affects. The patient's concern about emotional instability and its impact on her relationships might suggest borderline personality disorder. However, the clear relationship of symptoms to the menstrual cycle and the absence of other typical borderline personality disorder symptoms (e.g., chronic feelings of emptiness, fear of abandonment) make this diagnosis less likely than premenstrual dysphoric disorder.

To confirm the diagnosis of premenstrual dysphoric disorder, it would be helpful to have the patient keep a symptom diary for at least two menstrual cycles to document the timing and severity of symptoms. Treatment options for premenstrual dysphoric disorder include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, which can be used either continuously or only during the luteal phase, as well as hormonal contraceptives and lifestyle modifications.
learningPoints: Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is characterised by severe mood symptoms that occur in the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle and resolve with menses.
At least five symptoms, including one mood symptom, must be present for a diagnosis of premenstrual dysphoric disorder.
Premenstrual dysphoric disorder symptoms must significantly interfere with work, school, usual social activities, or relationships with others.
references: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Premenstrual syndrome: diagnostic criteria for premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Clinical Knowledge Summary. 2024. Available at: https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/premenstrual-syndrome/diagnosis/diagnostic-criteria-for-premenstrual-dysphoric-disorder/

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Premenstrual syndrome: management (Green-top Guideline No. 48). 2023. Available at: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/premenstrual-syndrome-management-green-top-guideline-no-48/
American Psychiatric Association DS, American Psychiatric Association DS. Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders: DSM-5. Washington, DC: American psychiatric association; 2013 May 22.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
